active.description: Column names should follow a single naming convention throughout a table (e.g. all UPPER_SNAKE_CASE). Mixing styles makes queries harder to write and datasets harder to join.
active.name: Inconsistent Column Naming Style
active.reason: Naming consistency remains a valid table-level concern.

active.description: Tables should have an assigned owner for accountability
active.name: Missing Table Owner
active.reason: Ownership accountability should continue to be tracked.

active.description: Mutable tables should track the last modification time via UPDATED_AT, MODIFIED_DATE, or equivalent. Required for CDC, incremental ETL, and auditing.
active.name: Missing Row Updated Timestamp
active.reason: LAST_UPDATED exists but a dedicated updated-timestamp convention check is still meaningful.

active.description: Columns ending in _ID, _KEY, _FK, or _PK should use a numeric type (NUMBER, INTEGER, BIGINT). Storing them as VARCHAR causes implicit conversions and silent join failures.
active.name: Column Type Mismatch — ID/key column should be numeric
active.reason: ID columns are numeric here, but the guard is cheap to keep active.

active.description: Columns whose names suggest personally identifiable information (e.g. EMAIL, SSN, PHONE, PASSWORD, DOB, SALARY) should have a Snowflake Dynamic Data Masking policy applied and a PII tag attached.
active.name: Potential PII Column Without Masking Policy
active.reason: PII masking scan should remain in case sensitive columns are added.

active.description: Columns ending in _DATE, _DT, or _DAY should use DATE or TIMESTAMP. Storing them as other types prevents date arithmetic and proper sorting.
active.name: Column Type Mismatch — Date column should be DATE or TIMESTAMP
active.reason: Date-type enforcement is a valid ongoing schema check.

active.description: Columns ending in '_ID' typically reference another table. Add an unenforced REFERENCES clause for documentation and data lineage tools.
active.name: Foreign Key Column Without FK Constraint
active.reason: SUPPLIER_ID is a FK-shaped column that would benefit from an FK constraint.

active.description: Columns whose names suggest a date or timestamp are stored as VARCHAR. This prevents date arithmetic, sorting, filtering, and indexing. Convert to DATE or TIMESTAMP.
active.name: Date/Timestamp Column Stored as VARCHAR
active.reason: Timestamp-as-VARCHAR guard is inexpensive and valid.

active.description: Primary key and identifier columns should never be NULL. A nullable PK column breaks referential integrity and causes unexpected results in GROUP BY, JOIN, and deduplication.
active.name: Nullable ID / Primary Key Column
active.reason: PRODUCT_ID is the key and should never be null.

active.description: Columns whose names suggest a boolean or flag value (_FL, _FLAG, _IND, IS_, _YN) are stored as VARCHAR. This allows invalid values and prevents efficient filtering. Use BOOLEAN or a small integer type.
active.name: Boolean/Flag Column Stored as VARCHAR
active.reason: Boolean-as-VARCHAR guard remains valid for IS_ACTIVE.

active.description: Tables should have descriptive comments explaining their purpose
active.name: Missing Table Comment
active.reason: Table comment documentation is still needed.

active.description: Tables with more than 50 columns often indicate poor normalisation, merged business entities, or accumulated technical debt. Consider decomposing into focused, related tables.
active.name: Table Has Too Many Columns
active.reason: Column-count check is harmless to keep though this table is small.

active.description: Columns should have descriptive comments explaining their data
active.name: Missing Column Comment
active.reason: Column comment documentation is still needed.

active.description: Column names like COL1, DATA, VALUE, FIELD, or MISC provide no semantic context. Rename them to describe what they actually store.
active.name: Generic / Uninformative Column Name
active.reason: Generic-name scan remains a valid naming guard.

active.description: Production tables should track when rows were inserted via a column such as CREATED_AT, CREATE_DATE, or INSERT_TS. This enables auditing, incremental loads, and change tracking.
active.name: Missing Row Creation Timestamp

active.description: Flags duplicate non-null values in a column expected to be unique (e.g. a primary/business key). The specific column/table and why it matters are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Uniqueness Violation
active.reason: SUPPLIER_ID matches common primary-key naming conventions but its live data has 12 duplicated value(s) — a real integrity risk for joins, GROUP BY, and deduplication.

active.description: Flags duplicate non-null values in a column expected to be unique (e.g. a primary/business key). The specific column/table and why it matters are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Uniqueness Violation
active.reason: PRODUCT_ID is the primary business key (20 distinct / 20 non-null); enforcing uniqueness prevents future duplicate catalog entries.

active.description: Flags a table whose most recent timestamp/date column value is older than an expected freshness threshold. The specific column/table/threshold are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Stale Data / Freshness Violation
active.reason: A product catalog that hasn't been updated in over two years likely indicates a stalled feed or abandoned pipeline.

active.description: Flags rows where a column expected to always be populated is NULL. The specific column/table and why it matters are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Not-Null Constraint Violation
active.reason: A product catalog entry with no name is unusable downstream for display, search, and ordering.

skipped.description: No column matching common primary-key naming patterns (ID, *_ID, PK_*, *_PK, *_KEY, *_SEQ) was found. Tables without a primary key risk duplicate rows and make joins, deduplication, and CDC harder.
skipped.name: Table May Be Missing a Primary Key
skipped.reason: PRODUCT_ID clearly serves as the primary key so this check is a false positive here.

output.table_type_reason: PRODUCT_CATALOG holds descriptive product records keyed by a unique PRODUCT_ID with categories, prices and supplier references, typical of a reference/dimension catalog.
